questions.prompt: If \(3x^2+2xy+y^2=2\), then the value of \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) at \(x=1\) is

questions.prompt: A continuous function \(f\) has a relative maximum at \((-1,4)\) and a relative minimum at \((3,-2)\). Which statement must be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The graph has a point of inflection somewhere between \(x=-1\) and \(x=3\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The graph has a horizontal asymptote.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The graph has a horizontal tangent at \(x=3\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: The graph intersects both coordinate axes.

questions.explanation: Continuity on \([-1,3]\) and the values \(4\) and \(-2\) guarantee an \(x\)-intercept by the IVT. Since \(f\) is defined for all real \(x\), it also has the point \((0,f(0))\) on the \(y\)-axis. Thus it intersects both axes.

questions.noncalculatorSolution: Continuity on \([-1,3]\) and the values \(4\) and \(-2\) guarantee an \(x\)-intercept by the IVT. Since \(f\) is defined for all real \(x\), it also has the point \((0,f(0))\) on the \(y\)-axis. Thus it intersects both axes.

questions.prompt:
Consider the following functions, defined for all real \(x\):
\[
f_1(x)=x,\qquad f_2(x)=x\cos x,\qquad f_3(x)=3e^{2x},\qquad f_4(x)=x-|x|.
\]
For each row below, answer Yes or No for every function. No justification is required in the historical question.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Does \(f(-x)=-f(x)\)? Give four answers in the order \(f_1,f_2,f_3,f_4\).
parts.answer: \(f_1:\text{ Yes},\ f_2:\text{ Yes},\ f_3:\text{ No},\ f_4:\text{ No}.\) The first two are odd. The exponential is not odd, and \(x-|x|\) is zero for every \(x\ge0\), so it cannot have origin symmetry.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Does an inverse function exist?
parts.answer: \(f_1:\text{ Yes},\ f_2:\text{ No},\ f_3:\text{ Yes},\ f_4:\text{ No}.\) The identity and exponential are one-to-one. \(x\cos x\) is not one-to-one, and \(x-|x|=0\) for every \(x\ge0\).

rubric.description: 1 point for each correct entry: Yes, No, Yes, No.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for each correct entry: Yes, No, Yes, No.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Is the function periodic?
parts.answer: No for all four functions. Multiplication by \(x\) destroys the periodicity of \(\cos x\); the other three also have no positive period.

rubric.description: 1 point for each correct entry: No, No, No, No.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for each correct entry: No, No, No, No.

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Is the function continuous at \(x=0\)?
parts.answer: Yes for all four functions. In particular, \(x-|x|\) is piecewise \(2x\) for \(x<0\) and \(0\) for \(x\ge0\), with matching value and limits at the origin.

rubric.description: 1 point for each correct entry: Yes, Yes, Yes, Yes.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for each correct entry: Yes, Yes, Yes, Yes.

questions.prompt:
A particle moves along the \(x\)-axis so that its position at time \(t\) is
\[
x(t)=3t^4-16t^3+24t^2,\qquad -5\le t\le5.
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the velocity and acceleration as functions of \(t\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct velocity function.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct velocity function.

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct acceleration function.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct acceleration function.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: At what times is the particle at rest?
parts.answer: Solve \(v(t)=12t(t-2)^2=0\). The particle is at rest at \(t=0\) and \(t=2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for setting velocity equal to zero or using its factorization.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for setting velocity equal to zero or using its factorization.

rubric.description: 1 point for both times \(0\) and \(2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for both times \(0\) and \(2\).

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: At what times does the particle change direction? Justify using the sign of velocity.
parts.answer: The factor \((t-2)^2\) never changes sign. Thus the sign of \(v\) is the sign of \(t\): negative for \(t<0\), positive for \(t>0\) except for the isolated zero at \(t=2\). The particle changes direction only at \(t=0\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a valid velocity sign analysis.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a valid velocity sign analysis.

rubric.description: 1 point for concluding that the only direction change is at \(t=0\), not at \(t=2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for concluding that the only direction change is at \(t=0\), not at \(t=2\).

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Find the velocity when the acceleration is zero for the first time as \(t\) increases from \(-5\).
parts.answer:
Acceleration is zero at \(t=\tfrac23\) and \(t=2\). The first is \(t=\tfrac23\). Therefore
\[v\!\left(\frac23\right)=12\left(\frac23\right)\left(\frac23-2\right)^2=\frac{128}{9}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for solving \(a(t)=0\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for solving \(a(t)=0\).

rubric.description: 1 point for identifying the first time as \(t=\tfrac23\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying the first time as \(t=\tfrac23\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(v(\tfrac23)=\tfrac{128}{9}\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(v(\tfrac23)=\tfrac{128}{9}\).

questions.prompt:
Let
\[
f(x)=\frac1x+\ln x
\]
on the closed interval \([e^{-1},e]\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Determine the \(x\)-coordinates at which \(f\) has its absolute maximum and absolute minimum. Show the comparison.
parts.answer:
\[f'(x)=\frac{x-1}{x^2}.\]
The only interior critical point is \(x=1\), where the sign changes from negative to positive. Compare
\[f(e^{-1})=e-1,\qquad f(1)=1,\qquad f(e)=1+\frac1e.\]
Hence the absolute maximum occurs at \(x=e^{-1}\), and the absolute minimum occurs at \(x=1\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the derivative and critical point \(x=1\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the derivative and critical point \(x=1\).

rubric.description: 1 point for evaluating the critical point and both endpoints.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for evaluating the critical point and both endpoints.

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct absolute maximum and minimum locations.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct absolute maximum and minimum locations.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: For what values of \(x\) is the graph concave up?
parts.answer: \[f''(x)=\frac{2-x}{x^3}.\] Since \(x>0\) on the interval, \(f''(x)>0\) exactly when \(x<2\). Thus the graph is concave up on \((e^{-1},2)\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct second derivative or equivalent sign expression.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct second derivative or equivalent sign expression.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Sketch the graph of \(f\) on \([e^{-1},e]\), showing the absolute extrema and the change in concavity.
parts.answer: The curve decreases from \((e^{-1},e-1)\) to the absolute minimum \((1,1)\), then increases to \((e,1+e^{-1})\). It is concave up until \(x=2\), has an inflection point at \((2,\tfrac12+\ln2)\), and is concave down thereafter.

rubric.description: 1 point for correct increasing/decreasing behavior and extrema.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for correct increasing/decreasing behavior and extrema.

rubric.description: 1 point for correct concavity and inflection behavior.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for correct concavity and inflection behavior.

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: The average value of \(f\) on the interval is \(\dfrac{2}{e-1}\). State a geometrical interpretation of this number relative to the graph.
parts.answer: The horizontal line \(y=\dfrac{2}{e-1}\) is the height of a rectangle over \([e^{-1},e]\) having the same area as the region under the graph of \(f\) on that interval.

rubric.description: 1 point for identifying the number as the average height/ordinate.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying the number as the average height/ordinate.

rubric.description: 1 point for the equal-area rectangle interpretation.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the equal-area rectangle interpretation.

questions.prompt:
The number of bacteria in a culture at time \(t\) is approximated by
\[
N(t)=1000\left(25+t e^{-t/20}\right),\qquad 0\le t\le100.
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the largest and smallest numbers of bacteria during the interval, and state when they occur.
parts.answer:
\[N'(t)=1000e^{-t/20}\left(1-\frac{t}{20}\right).\]
The only interior critical time is \(t=20\). Compare
\[N(0)=25000,\quad N(20)=25000+\frac{20000}{e},\quad N(100)=25000+\frac{100000}{e^5}.\]
Thus the largest number is \(25000+\dfrac{20000}{e}\) at \(t=20\), and the smallest number is \(25000\) at \(t=0\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct derivative.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct derivative.

rubric.description: 1 point for the critical time \(t=20\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the critical time \(t=20\).

rubric.description: 1 point for evaluating both endpoints and the critical point.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for evaluating both endpoints and the critical point.

rubric.description: 1 point for the maximum value and time.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the maximum value and time.

rubric.description: 1 point for the minimum value and time.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the minimum value and time.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: At what time is the rate of change in the number of bacteria a minimum? Give the minimum rate.
parts.answer:
We minimize \(N'(t)\). Its derivative is
\[N''(t)=\frac{1000}{400}e^{-t/20}(t-40).\]
Therefore \(N'\) decreases until \(t=40\) and increases afterward. The minimum rate occurs at \(t=40\), and
\[N'(40)=-\frac{1000}{e^2}\ \text{bacteria per unit time}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for differentiating the rate to obtain \(N''\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for differentiating the rate to obtain \(N''\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the critical time \(t=40\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the critical time \(t=40\).

rubric.description: 1 point for verifying a minimum of \(N'\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for verifying a minimum of \(N'\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the minimum rate \(-1000/e^2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the minimum rate \(-1000/e^2\).

questions.prompt:
Let \(R\) be the region enclosed by the graphs
\[
y=x^2\qquad\text{and}\qquad y=2x.
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the area of \(R\).
parts.answer:
The curves intersect where \(x^2=2x\), so \(x=0,2\). On this interval the line is above the parabola:
\[A=\int_0^2(2x-x^2)\,dx=\left[x^2-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^2=\frac43.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the intersection values \(0\) and \(2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the intersection values \(0\) and \(2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct top-minus-bottom integrand.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct top-minus-bottom integrand.

rubric.description: 1 point for correct limits.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for correct limits.

rubric.description: 1 point for \(A=4/3\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(A=4/3\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the volume obtained by revolving \(R\) about the \(y\)-axis.
parts.answer:
Using washers with respect to \(y\), \(0\le y\le4\), the outer radius is \(\sqrt y\) and inner radius is \(y/2\):
\[V=\pi\int_0^4\left[(\sqrt y)^2-\left(\frac y2\right)^2\right]dy
=\pi\int_0^4\left(y-\frac{y^2}{4}\right)dy=\frac{8\pi}{3}.\]
Equivalently, cylindrical shells give \(2\pi\int_0^2x(2x-x^2)\,dx\).

rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a valid washer or shell method.

questions.prompt: An arched window has base width \(2b\) and height \(h\). The arch is either a parabolic arc or one half-cycle of a cosine curve. Its endpoints are \((-b,0)\) and \((b,0)\), and its highest point is \((0,h)\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Write an equation for the parabolic arch.
parts.answer:
By symmetry write \(y=h+Ax^2\). Using \(y(b)=0\) gives \(A=-h/b^2\). Thus
\[y=h\left(1-\frac{x^2}{b^2}\right),\qquad -b\le x\le b.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for a symmetric quadratic model.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a symmetric quadratic model.

rubric.description: 1 point for using the endpoint condition.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for using the endpoint condition.

rubric.description: 1 point for \(y=h(1-x^2/b^2)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(y=h(1-x^2/b^2)\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write an equation for the half-cycle cosine arch.
parts.answer:
The cosine should have maximum \(h\) at \(x=0\) and zeros at \(x=\pm b\). Therefore
\[y=h\cos\left(\frac{\pi x}{2b}\right),\qquad -b\le x\le b.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for amplitude \(h\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for amplitude \(h\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a cosine maximum at the center.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a cosine maximum at the center.

rubric.description: 1 point for angular factor \(\pi/(2b)\) and the stated equation.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for angular factor \(\pi/(2b)\) and the stated equation.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Which design has greater area? Justify by finding both areas.
parts.answer:
\[A_P=\int_{-b}^{b}h\left(1-\frac{x^2}{b^2}\right)dx=\frac{4bh}{3},\]
\[A_C=\int_{-b}^{b}h\cos\left(\frac{\pi x}{2b}\right)dx=\frac{4bh}{\pi}.\]
Since \(\pi>3\), \(\frac1{3}>\frac1\pi\), so the parabolic window has greater area.

rubric.description: 1 point for the parabolic area \(4bh/3\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the parabolic area \(4bh/3\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the cosine area \(4bh/\pi\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the cosine area \(4bh/\pi\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct comparison and justification.

questions.prompt:
Consider the curve
\[
y=\frac{e^x+e^{-x}}{2}.
\]
Let \(R\) be the point on the curve whose \(x\)-coordinate is \(r\), where \(r\ne0\). The tangent line at \(R\) intersects the \(x\)-axis at \(Q\). Let \(P=(r,0)\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Sketch the graph of the curve.
parts.answer: The graph is even, passes through \((0,1)\), has a horizontal tangent and absolute minimum at \((0,1)\), is concave up everywhere, and grows without bound as \(x\to\pm\infty\).

rubric.description: 1 point for symmetry, the point \((0,1)\), and the minimum.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for symmetry, the point \((0,1)\), and the minimum.

rubric.description: 1 point for correct upward growth and concavity.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for correct upward growth and concavity.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the coordinates of \(Q\).
parts.answer:
At \(x=r\),
\[R=\left(r,\frac{e^r+e^{-r}}2\right),\qquad m=\frac{e^r-e^{-r}}2.\]
The tangent equation is
\[y-\frac{e^r+e^{-r}}2=\frac{e^r-e^{-r}}2(x-r).\]
Setting \(y=0\) gives
\[Q=\left(r-\frac{e^r+e^{-r}}{e^r-e^{-r}},\ 0\right).\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the derivative.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the derivative.

rubric.description: 1 point for the tangent-line equation at \(r\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the tangent-line equation at \(r\).

rubric.description: 1 point for setting \(y=0\) and solving for the intercept.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for setting \(y=0\) and solving for the intercept.

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct coordinates of \(Q\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct coordinates of \(Q\).

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find \(PQ\) as a function of \(r\), and find its limiting value as \(r\to\infty\).
parts.answer:
Since \(P=(r,0)\),
\[PQ=\left|r-\left(r-\frac{e^r+e^{-r}}{e^r-e^{-r}}\right)\right|=\left|\frac{e^r+e^{-r}}{e^r-e^{-r}}\right|.\]
For \(r\to\infty\), divide numerator and denominator by \(e^r\):
\[\lim_{r\to\infty}PQ=\lim_{r\to\infty}\frac{1+e^{-2r}}{1-e^{-2r}}=1.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the distance expression.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the distance expression.

questions.prompt:
Consider the parabola
\[
y=x^2-2x+3.
\]
At the point \((2,3)\), let \(L\) be the line perpendicular to the tangent line. In the first quadrant, let \(R\) be the region lying below both the parabola and \(L\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find an equation of the normal line \(L\) at \((2,3)\).
parts.answer:
The derivative is
\[
y'=2x-2,
\]
so the tangent slope at \(x=2\) is \(2\). The normal slope is therefore \(-\tfrac12\). Using \((2,3)\),
\[
y-3=-\frac12(x-2),
\qquad\boxed{y=4-\frac{x}{2}}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the tangent slope \(2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the tangent slope \(2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the perpendicular slope \(-\tfrac12\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the perpendicular slope \(-\tfrac12\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct normal-line equation.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct normal-line equation.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the area of \(R\). Show the intersection and the integral setup.
parts.answer:
Solve the first-quadrant intersection:
\[
x^2-2x+3=4-\frac{x}{2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad 2x^2-3x-2=0,
\]
so \(x=2\) or \(x=-\tfrac12\). The relevant intersection is \(x=2\). The line meets the \(x\)-axis at \(x=8\). On \([0,2]\), the parabola is lower; on \([2,8]\), the line is lower. Thus
\[
A=\int_0^2(x^2-2x+3)\,dx+\int_2^8\left(4-\frac{x}{2}\right)\,dx.
\]
The two contributions are \(\tfrac{14}{3}\) and \(9\), so
\[
\boxed{A=\frac{41}{3}}.
\]

rubric.description: 2 points for the correct piecewise definite-integral setup.

questions.prompt:

parts.label: (a)

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the coordinates of every point where the tangent line is horizontal.
parts.answer:
For \(x\ne0\),
\[
f'(x)=\frac43x^{1/3}+\frac43x^{-2/3}=\frac{4(x+1)}{3x^{2/3}}.
\]
A horizontal tangent requires \(f'(x)=0\), so \(x=-1\). Since \(f(-1)=1-4=-3\), the point is
\[
\boxed{(-1,-3)}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for solving \(f'(x)=0\) to get \(x=-1\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for solving \(f'(x)=0\) to get \(x=-1\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the point \((-1,-3)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the point \((-1,-3)\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the coordinates of every point where the tangent line is vertical.
parts.answer:
At \(x=0\), the function is continuous and
\[
f'(x)=\frac{4(x+1)}{3x^{2/3}}\longrightarrow+\infty
\]
from both sides. Therefore the graph has a vertical tangent at
\[
\boxed{(0,0)}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for identifying the derivative’s unbounded behavior at \(x=0\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying the derivative’s unbounded behavior at \(x=0\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the point \((0,0)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the point \((0,0)\).

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find the coordinates at which the absolute maximum and absolute minimum occur on \([-8,8]\).
parts.answer:
Check the endpoints, the critical point \(x=-1\), and the nondifferentiable interior point \(x=0\):
\[
f(-8)=8,\quad f(-1)=-3,\quad f(0)=0,\quad f(8)=24.
\]
Hence the absolute minimum is \(\boxed{(-1,-3)}\), and the absolute maximum is \(\boxed{(8,24)}\).

rubric.description: 1 point for listing all required candidates, including \(x=0\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for listing all required candidates, including \(x=0\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the absolute minimum \((-1,-3)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the absolute minimum \((-1,-3)\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the absolute maximum \((8,24)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the absolute maximum \((8,24)\).

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: For what values of \(x\) is the graph concave downward?
parts.answer:
For \(x\ne0\),
\[
f''(x)=\frac{4(x-2)}{9x^{5/3}}.
\]
This is negative exactly when \(0<x<2\). Therefore
\[
\boxed{\text{concave down on }(0,2)}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct second derivative or equivalent sign analysis.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct second derivative or equivalent sign analysis.

rubric.description: 1 point for \((0,2)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \((0,2)\).

parts.label: (e)
parts.prompt: Sketch the graph on \([-8,8]\), marking the important points and behavior found above.
parts.answer: The graph decreases from \((-8,8)\) to the horizontal-tangent absolute minimum \((-1,-3)\), then increases through the vertical tangent \((0,0)\) and continues to the absolute maximum \((8,24)\). It is concave up on \((-8,0)\), concave down on \((0,2)\), and concave up on \((2,8)\). Under the modern concavity-change convention, \(x=0\) and \(x=2\) are inflection abscissas; \(f(2)=6\sqrt[3]{2}\).

questions.prompt: A right circular cone and a hemisphere have the same circular base, and the cone is inscribed in the hemisphere. The figure expands so that the combined surface area of the hemisphere and its base increases at a constant rate of \(18\) square inches per second. Find the rate at which the cone’s volume is changing when the common radius is \(4\) inches.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Express the total area in terms of \(r\), and find \(dr/dt\) when \(r=4\).
parts.answer:
The curved hemisphere has area \(2\pi r^2\), and its base has area \(\pi r^2\). Thus
\[
S=3\pi r^2.
\]
Differentiate:
\[
\frac{dS}{dt}=6\pi r\frac{dr}{dt}=18.
\]
At \(r=4\),
\[
\boxed{\frac{dr}{dt}=\frac{3}{4\pi}\text{ in/s}}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for \(S=3\pi r^2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(S=3\pi r^2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for differentiating with respect to time.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for differentiating with respect to time.

rubric.description: 1 point for substituting \(dS/dt=18\) and \(r=4\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for substituting \(dS/dt=18\) and \(r=4\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(dr/dt=3/(4\pi)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(dr/dt=3/(4\pi)\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Use the cone’s geometry to find \(dV/dt\) at that instant.
parts.answer:
For a cone inscribed in the hemisphere with the same base, the cone height equals the hemisphere radius: \(h=r\). Hence
\[
V=\frac13\pi r^2h=\frac13\pi r^3.
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac{dV}{dt}=\pi r^2\frac{dr}{dt}.
\]
At \(r=4\),
\[
\frac{dV}{dt}=\pi(16)\left(\frac{3}{4\pi}\right)=\boxed{12\text{ in}^3/\text{s}}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for recognizing \(h=r\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for recognizing \(h=r\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(V=\frac13\pi r^3\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(V=\frac13\pi r^3\).

questions.prompt:
A particle moves along the \(x\)-axis so that, for \(t>0\), its position is
\[
x(t)=\sin(e^t).
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the velocity and acceleration as functions of \(t\).
parts.answer:
By the chain and product rules,
\[
v(t)=x'(t)=e^t\cos(e^t),
\]
\[
a(t)=v'(t)=e^t\cos(e^t)-e^{2t}\sin(e^t).
\]

rubric.description: 2 points for \(v(t)=e^t\cos(e^t)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 2 points for \(v(t)=e^t\cos(e^t)\).

rubric.description: 2 points for \(a(t)=e^t\cos(e^t)-e^{2t}\sin(e^t)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 2 points for \(a(t)=e^t\cos(e^t)-e^{2t}\sin(e^t)\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: At what time does the particle first have zero velocity?
parts.answer:
Because \(e^t>0\), velocity is zero when \(\cos(e^t)=0\). Since \(t>0\), \(e^t>1\), and the first positive cosine zero reached is \(e^t=\frac{\pi}{2}\). Therefore
\[
\boxed{t=\ln\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for reducing to \(\cos(e^t)=0\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for reducing to \(\cos(e^t)=0\).

rubric.description: 1 point for choosing the first admissible value \(e^t=\pi/2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for choosing the first admissible value \(e^t=\pi/2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(t=\ln(\pi/2)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(t=\ln(\pi/2)\).

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find the acceleration at the time from part (b).
parts.answer:
At \(e^t=\frac{\pi}{2}\), \(\cos(e^t)=0\) and \(\sin(e^t)=1\). Hence
\[
a=-\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^2=\boxed{-\frac{\pi^2}{4}}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for substituting the trigonometric values at \(e^t=\pi/2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for substituting the trigonometric values at \(e^t=\pi/2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(-\pi^2/4\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(-\pi^2/4\).

questions.prompt:
A parabola \(P\) is symmetric about the \(y\)-axis and passes through
\[
(0,0)\quad\text{and}\quad \left(b,e^{-b^2}\right),\qquad b>0.
\]
The region bounded by \(P\) and the horizontal line \(y=e^{-b^2}\) is revolved about the \(y\)-axis to form a solid \(F\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Write an equation for \(P\).
parts.answer:
A parabola symmetric about the \(y\)-axis with vertex at the origin has form \(y=ax^2\). Substituting \((b,e^{-b^2})\) gives
\[
a=\frac{e^{-b^2}}{b^2},
\]
so
\[
\boxed{y=\frac{e^{-b^2}}{b^2}x^2}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for using the form \(y=ax^2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for using the form \(y=ax^2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(a=e^{-b^2}/b^2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(a=e^{-b^2}/b^2\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Compute the volume of \(F\) as a function of \(b\).
parts.answer:
Using cylindrical shells, a vertical shell has radius \(x\) and height
\[
e^{-b^2}-\frac{e^{-b^2}}{b^2}x^2=e^{-b^2}\left(1-\frac{x^2}{b^2}\right).
\]
Thus
\[
V=2\pi e^{-b^2}\int_0^b x\left(1-\frac{x^2}{b^2}\right)dx
=\boxed{\frac{\pi}{2}b^2e^{-b^2}}.
\]
A washer integral with respect to \(y\) gives the same result.

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct radius and shell height or equivalent washer radius.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct radius and shell height or equivalent washer radius.

rubric.description: 1 point for correct bounds.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for correct bounds.

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct volume integral.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct volume integral.

rubric.description: 1 point for \(V=(\pi/2)b^2e^{-b^2}\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(V=(\pi/2)b^2e^{-b^2}\).

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: For what value of \(b\) is the volume maximum? Justify.
parts.answer:
Differentiate:
\[
V'(b)=\pi b e^{-b^2}(1-b^2).
\]
For \(b>0\), the only critical value is \(b=1\). The derivative is positive for \(0<b<1\) and negative for \(b>1\), so the volume is maximized at
\[
\boxed{b=1}.
\]
The maximum volume is \(\pi/(2e)\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct derivative.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct derivative.

rubric.description: 1 point for the critical value \(b=1\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the critical value \(b=1\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a valid maximum justification.

questions.prompt:
Given that
\[
\sin t\le t\qquad\text{for all }t\ge0,
\]
use repeated integration to prove that, for every \(x\ge0\),
\[
1-\frac{x^2}{2!}\le\cos x\le1-\frac{x^2}{2!}+\frac{x^4}{4!}.
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Derive the lower bound \(1-\frac{x^2}{2}\le\cos x\).
parts.answer:
Integrate the given inequality from \(0\) to \(x\):
\[
\int_0^x\sin t\,dt\le\int_0^x t\,dt.
\]
Because \(x\ge0\), the order is preserved. Therefore
\[
1-\cos x\le\frac{x^2}{2},
\]
which rearranges to
\[
\boxed{1-\frac{x^2}{2}\le\cos x}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for integrating over \([0,x]\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for integrating over \([0,x]\).

rubric.description: 1 point for preserving the inequality direction.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for preserving the inequality direction.

rubric.description: 1 point for \(\int_0^x\sin t\,dt=1-\cos x\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(\int_0^x\sin t\,dt=1-\cos x\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the polynomial integral \(x^2/2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the polynomial integral \(x^2/2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the correctly rearranged lower bound.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correctly rearranged lower bound.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Use the lower bound and two further integrations to derive the upper bound for cosine.
parts.answer:
From part (a), for every \(u\ge0\),
\[
\cos u\ge1-\frac{u^2}{2}.
\]
Integrate from \(0\) to \(t\):
\[
\sin t=\int_0^t\cos u\,du\ge t-\frac{t^3}{6}.
\]
Now integrate from \(0\) to \(x\):
\[
1-\cos x=\int_0^x\sin t\,dt
\ge\frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{x^4}{24}.
\]
Rearranging gives
\[
\boxed{\cos x\le1-\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{x^4}{24}}
=\boxed{1-\frac{x^2}{2!}+\frac{x^4}{4!}}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for applying the lower cosine bound with a fresh variable.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for applying the lower cosine bound with a fresh variable.

questions.prompt:
Let
\[
f(x)=\ln x \quad (x>0),
\qquad g(x)=x^2-4 \quad (x\in\mathbb R).
\]
Define the compositions
\[
H(x)=f(g(x))
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
K(x)=g(f(x)).
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the domain of \(H\).
parts.answer:
Since
\[
H(x)=\ln(x^2-4),
\]
we require \(x^2-4>0\). Thus \(x^2>4\), so
\[
\boxed{\operatorname{Dom}(H)=(-\infty,-2)\cup(2,\infty)}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for requiring \(x^2-4>0\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for requiring \(x^2-4>0\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct union of intervals.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct union of intervals.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the range of \(H\).
parts.answer:
On its domain, \(x^2-4\) assumes every positive value. The natural logarithm maps \((0,\infty)\) onto all real numbers. Therefore
\[
\boxed{\operatorname{Range}(H)=(-\infty,\infty)}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for recognizing that \(x^2-4\) ranges over all positive values.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for recognizing that \(x^2-4\) ranges over all positive values.

rubric.description: 1 point for the range \(\mathbb R\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the range \(\mathbb R\).

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find the domain of \(K\).
parts.answer:
Because
\[
K(x)=(\ln x)^2-4,
\]
the only restriction comes from \(\ln x\), which requires \(x>0\). Hence
\[
\boxed{\operatorname{Dom}(K)=(0,\infty)}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for identifying the logarithmic restriction.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying the logarithmic restriction.

rubric.description: 1 point for \((0,\infty)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \((0,\infty)\).

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Find the range of \(K\).
parts.answer:
As \(x\) ranges over \((0,\infty)\), \(\ln x\) ranges over all real numbers. Therefore \((\ln x)^2\ge 0\), with equality at \(x=1\), and it is unbounded above. Thus
\[
\boxed{\operatorname{Range}(K)=[-4,\infty)}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the minimum value \(-4\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the minimum value \(-4\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the complete interval \([-4,\infty)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the complete interval \([-4,\infty)\).

parts.label: (e)
parts.answer:
By the chain rule,
\[
H'(x)=\frac{1}{x^2-4}(2x)=\frac{2x}{x^2-4}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{H'(7)=\frac{14}{45}}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for \(H'(x)=\frac{2x}{x^2-4}\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(H'(x)=\frac{2x}{x^2-4}\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(\frac{14}{45}\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(\frac{14}{45}\).

questions.prompt:
Let \(R\) be the region in the first quadrant that lies below both curves
\[
y=3x^2
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
y=\frac{3}{x},
\]
and to the left of the line \(x=k\), where \(k>1\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the area \(A\) of \(R\) as a function of \(k\).
parts.answer:
The curves meet where
\[
3x^2=\frac3x
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad x^3=1,
\]
so the first-quadrant intersection is \(x=1\). On \([0,1]\), the lower curve is \(3x^2\); on \([1,k]\), it is \(3/x\). Hence
\[
A(k)=\int_0^1 3x^2\,dx+\int_1^k\frac3x\,dx
=1+3\ln k.
\]
Thus
\[
\boxed{A(k)=1+3\ln k},\qquad k>1.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the intersection \(x=1\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the intersection \(x=1\).

rubric.description: 1 point for identifying the lower curve on each interval.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying the lower curve on each interval.

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct piecewise integral.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct piecewise integral.

rubric.description: 1 point for \(A(k)=1+3\ln k\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(A(k)=1+3\ln k\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: When the area of \(R\) is \(7\), find \(k\).
parts.answer:
Set the area equal to \(7\):
\[
1+3\ln k=7
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\ln k=2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{k=e^2}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the equation \(1+3\ln k=7\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the equation \(1+3\ln k=7\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(\ln k=2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(\ln k=2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(k=e^2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(k=e^2\).

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: The area is increasing at a constant rate of \(5\) square units per second. At what rate is \(k\) increasing when \(k=15\)?
parts.answer:
Differentiate \(A=1+3\ln k\) with respect to time:
\[
\frac{dA}{dt}=\frac3k\frac{dk}{dt}.
\]
At \(k=15\) and \(dA/dt=5\),
\[
5=\frac3{15}\frac{dk}{dt}.
\]
Hence
\[
\boxed{\frac{dk}{dt}=25\text{ units per second}}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for \(\frac{dA}{dt}=\frac3k\frac{dk}{dt}\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(\frac{dA}{dt}=\frac3k\frac{dk}{dt}\).

rubric.description: 1 point for substituting the stated values.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for substituting the stated values.

rubric.description: 1 point for \(25\) with appropriate units.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(25\) with appropriate units.

questions.prompt:
Consider
\[
F(x)=\cos^2x+2\cos x
\]
over one complete period beginning with \(x=0\); use \(0\le x\le 2\pi\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find all values of \(x\) in this period for which \(F(x)=0\).
parts.answer:
Factor:
\[
F(x)=\cos x(\cos x+2).
\]
Since \(\cos x=-2\) is impossible, we need \(\cos x=0\). Therefore
\[
\boxed{x=\frac\pi2,\;\frac{3\pi}{2}}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for factoring or an equivalent substitution.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for factoring or an equivalent substitution.

rubric.description: 1 point for rejecting \(\cos x=-2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for rejecting \(\cos x=-2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for both zeros in the stated period.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for both zeros in the stated period.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find all values of \(x\) in this period at which \(F\) has a minimum. Justify your answer.
parts.answer:
Complete the square:
\[
F(x)=(\cos x+1)^2-1.
\]
Because a square is nonnegative, the smallest possible value is \(-1\), attained when \(\cos x=-1\). In the stated period this occurs at
\[
\boxed{x=\pi},
\qquad F(\pi)=-1.
\]
This is the absolute minimum on the period.

rubric.description: 1 point for rewriting or otherwise identifying the minimum value.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for rewriting or otherwise identifying the minimum value.

rubric.description: 1 point for the condition \(\cos x=-1\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the condition \(\cos x=-1\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(x=\pi\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(x=\pi\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a valid absolute-minimum justification.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a valid absolute-minimum justification.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: On what interval or intervals in this period is the graph concave up?
parts.answer:
Differentiate twice:
\[
F'(x)=-2\sin x(\cos x+1),
\]
\[
F''(x)=-2(2\cos^2x+\cos x-1)
=-2(2\cos x-1)(\cos x+1).
\]
Since \(\cos x+1\ge0\), the second derivative is positive when \(\cos x<\tfrac12\), except that it equals zero at the isolated point \(x=\pi\). Thus the graph is concave up throughout
\[
\boxed{\left(\frac\pi3,\frac{5\pi}{3}\right)}.
\]
The isolated zero of \(F''\) at \(x=\pi\) does not produce a change in concavity.

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct second derivative.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct second derivative.

rubric.description: 1 point for solving the sign condition.

questions.prompt:
Find the area of the largest rectangle, with sides parallel to the coordinate axes, that can be inscribed in the region enclosed by
\[
f(x)=18-x^2
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
g(x)=2x^2-9.
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Determine the dimensions and area of the largest such rectangle. Justify that the area is maximal.
parts.answer:
The curves intersect where
\[
18-x^2=2x^2-9,
\]
so \(x=\pm3\). By symmetry, let the vertical sides of the rectangle be \(x=\pm a\), where \(0\le a\le3\). Its width is \(2a\), and its height is
\[
(18-a^2)-(2a^2-9)=27-3a^2.
\]
Therefore
\[
A(a)=2a(27-3a^2)=54a-6a^3.
\]
Differentiate:
\[
A'(a)=54-18a^2=18(3-a^2).
\]
The positive critical value is \(a=\sqrt3\). Also,
\[
A''(a)=-36a<0
\]
at \(a=\sqrt3\), so this critical point gives a maximum. The dimensions are
\[
\text{width}=2\sqrt3,
\qquad
\text{height}=27-3(3)=18.
\]
Hence the largest area is
\[
\boxed{A_{\max}=36\sqrt3}.
\]

questions.prompt:
Let \(R\) be the first-quadrant region bounded by the \(x\)-axis and
\[
y=2x-x^2.
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the volume generated when \(R\) is revolved about the \(x\)-axis.
parts.answer:
The curve meets the \(x\)-axis at \(x=0\) and \(x=2\). Using disks,
\[
V_x=\pi\int_0^2(2x-x^2)^2\,dx.
\]
Since
\[
(2x-x^2)^2=4x^2-4x^3+x^4,
\]
\[
V_x=\pi\left[\frac{4x^3}{3}-x^4+\frac{x^5}{5}\right]_0^2
=\boxed{\frac{16\pi}{15}}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the bounds \(0\) and \(2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the bounds \(0\) and \(2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the disk radius \(2x-x^2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the disk radius \(2x-x^2\).

rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct integral.

rubric.description: 1 point for correct evaluation.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for correct evaluation.

rubric.description: 1 point for \(16\pi/15\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(16\pi/15\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the volume generated when \(R\) is revolved about the \(y\)-axis.
parts.answer:
A washer solution remains within current AP Calculus AB methods. Write
\[
y=1-(x-1)^2,
\]
so for \(0\le y\le1\), the outer and inner radii are
\[
R(y)=1+\sqrt{1-y},
\qquad r(y)=1-\sqrt{1-y}.
\]
Therefore
\[
V_y=\pi\int_0^1\left[(1+\sqrt{1-y})^2-(1-\sqrt{1-y})^2\right]dy.
\]
The bracket simplifies to \(4\sqrt{1-y}\), so
\[
V_y=4\pi\int_0^1\sqrt{1-y}\,dy
=\boxed{\frac{8\pi}{3}}.
\]
Equivalently, cylindrical shells give \(2\pi\int_0^2x(2x-x^2)\,dx\).

questions.prompt:
A particle starts at the point \((5,0)\) at \(t=0\) and moves along the \(x\)-axis. For \(t>0\), its velocity is
\[
v(t)=\frac{t}{1+t^2}.
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Determine the maximum velocity attained by the particle. Justify your answer.
parts.answer:
Differentiate:
\[
v'(t)=\frac{1-t^2}{(1+t^2)^2}.
\]
For \(t>0\), this derivative is positive on \((0,1)\) and negative on \((1,\infty)\). Thus the absolute maximum occurs at \(t=1\), with
\[
\boxed{v_{\max}=v(1)=\frac12}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the derivative.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the derivative.

rubric.description: 1 point for the increasing/decreasing sign analysis.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the increasing/decreasing sign analysis.

rubric.description: 1 point for the maximum value \(1/2\) at \(t=1\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the maximum value \(1/2\) at \(t=1\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Determine the position of the particle at \(t=6\).
parts.answer:
Position equals initial position plus accumulated velocity:
\[
x(t)=5+\int_0^t\frac{s}{1+s^2}\,ds
=5+\frac12\ln(1+t^2).
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{x(6)=5+\frac12\ln37}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for position as initial value plus the velocity integral.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for position as initial value plus the velocity integral.

rubric.description: 1 point for the antiderivative \(\frac12\ln(1+t^2)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the antiderivative \(\frac12\ln(1+t^2)\).

rubric.description: 1 point for \(5+\frac12\ln37\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(5+\frac12\ln37\).

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find the limiting value of the velocity as \(t\to\infty\).
parts.answer:
Divide numerator and denominator by \(t^2\):
\[
v(t)=\frac{1/t}{1/t^2+1}\longrightarrow0.
\]
Hence
\[
\boxed{\lim_{t\to\infty}v(t)=0}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for a valid limit argument.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a valid limit argument.

rubric.description: 1 point for the limit \(0\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the limit \(0\).

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Does the particle ever pass the point \((500,0)\)? Explain.
parts.answer:
Although the velocity tends to zero, the position
\[
x(t)=5+\frac12\ln(1+t^2)
\]
is unbounded because \(\ln(1+t^2)\to\infty\). Therefore the particle eventually passes every point to the right, including \(x=500\). In fact, the crossing time satisfies
\[
5+\frac12\ln(1+t^2)=500,
\]
so
\[
t=\sqrt{e^{990}-1},
\]
a finite but extremely large time. Thus \(
\boxed{\text{yes}}
\).

rubric.description: 1 point for recognizing that the position is unbounded.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for recognizing that the position is unbounded.

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct conclusion with a valid explanation.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct conclusion with a valid explanation.

questions.prompt:
Let
\[
f(x)=|x|^{1/2}e^{-x^2}
\]
for all real numbers \(x\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Describe the symmetry of the graph of \(f\).
parts.answer:
Because \(|-x|=|x|\) and \((-x)^2=x^2\),
\[
f(-x)=|-x|^{1/2}e^{-(-x)^2}=|x|^{1/2}e^{-x^2}=f(x).
\]
Therefore \(f\) is even and its graph is symmetric about the \(y\)-axis:
\[
\boxed{f(-x)=f(x)}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for computing or stating \(f(-x)=f(x)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for computing or stating \(f(-x)=f(x)\).

rubric.description: 1 point for symmetry about the \(y\)-axis.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for symmetry about the \(y\)-axis.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: On what intervals is \(f\) increasing?
parts.answer:
For \(x>0\),
\[
f(x)=x^{1/2}e^{-x^2},
\]
so
\[
f'(x)=e^{-x^2}\left(\frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}-2x^{3/2}\right)
=\frac{e^{-x^2}(1-4x^2)}{2\sqrt{x}}.
\]
Thus \(f'(x)>0\) for \(0<x<\tfrac12\) and \(f'(x)<0\) for \(x>\tfrac12\). Since \(f\) is even, its derivative behavior reflects across the \(y\)-axis. Therefore \(f\) is increasing on
\[
\boxed{(-\infty,-\tfrac12)\cup(0,\tfrac12)}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for differentiating correctly on \(x>0\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for differentiating correctly on \(x>0\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the sign change at \(x=1/2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for using even symmetry on the negative side.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Sketch the graph, showing clearly its behavior near the origin, all maximum and minimum points, and its behavior for large \(|x|\).
parts.answer:
The function is nonnegative and even. It satisfies \(f(0)=0\), but the one-sided slopes become unbounded in magnitude, producing a sharp minimum at the origin. The critical points away from the origin are \(x=\pm\tfrac12\). At both points,
\[
f\left(\pm\frac12\right)
=\sqrt{\frac12}e^{-1/4}
=\frac{e^{-1/4}}{\sqrt2}.
\]
They are equal absolute maxima. Finally,
\[
\lim_{|x|\to\infty}|x|^{1/2}e^{-x^2}=0,
\]
so the graph approaches the \(x\)-axis from above on both ends. The feature list is therefore
\[
\boxed{\text{minimum }(0,0),\quad
\text{maxima }\left(\pm\frac12,\frac{e^{-1/4}}{\sqrt2}\right),\quad
f(x)\to0.}
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the \(x\)-intercepts of the graph of \(f\).
parts.answer:
Factor the polynomial:
\[
4x^3-3x-1=(x-1)(4x^2+4x+1)=(x-1)(2x+1)^2.
\]
Thus the zeros are \(x=1\) and \(x=-\tfrac12\). Therefore the \(x\)-intercepts are
\[
\boxed{(1,0)\text{ and }\left(-\frac12,0\right)}.
\]
The intercept at \(x=-\tfrac12\) has even multiplicity, so the graph touches the axis there.

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct factorization or equivalent zero-finding method.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct factorization or equivalent zero-finding method.

rubric.description: 1 point for identifying \(x=1\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying \(x=1\).

rubric.description: 1 point for identifying \(x=-\tfrac12\) and giving the intercept coordinates.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying \(x=-\tfrac12\) and giving the intercept coordinates.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write an equation for the tangent line to the graph of \(f\) at \(x=2\).
parts.answer:
Differentiate:
\[
f'(x)=12x^2-3.
\]
At \(x=2\),
\[
f'(2)=45,\qquad f(2)=4(8)-6-1=25.
\]
Using point-slope form,
\[
y-25=45(x-2),
\]
so
\[
\boxed{y=45x-65}.
\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Write an equation for the graph obtained by reflecting the graph of \(f\) across the \(y\)-axis.
parts.answer:
Reflection across the \(y\)-axis replaces \(x\) by \(-x\):
\[
y=f(-x)=4(-x)^3-3(-x)-1.
\]
Hence
\[
\boxed{y=-4x^3+3x-1}.
\]

questions.prompt:
A particle starts at time \(t=0\) and moves on a number line so that its position at time \(t\) is
\[
x(t)=(t-2)^3(t-6),\qquad t\ge 0.
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: When is the particle moving to the right?
parts.answer:
Differentiate the position:
\[
v(t)=x'(t)=3(t-2)^2(t-6)+(t-2)^3=4(t-2)^2(t-5).
\]
Since \((t-2)^2\ge0\), the sign is controlled by \(t-5\), except at the zeros. Therefore \(v(t)>0\) when
\[
\boxed{t>5}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for \(t>5\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(t>5\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: When is the particle at rest?
parts.answer:
The particle is at rest when \(v(t)=0\):
\[
4(t-2)^2(t-5)=0.
\]
Thus
\[
\boxed{t=2\text{ and }t=5}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for setting the velocity equal to zero.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for setting the velocity equal to zero.

rubric.description: 1 point for both times \(2\) and \(5\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for both times \(2\) and \(5\).

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: When does the particle change direction?
parts.answer:
At \(t=2\), the factor \((t-2)^2\) has even multiplicity, so the velocity is negative on both sides and no direction change occurs. At \(t=5\), velocity changes from negative to positive. Therefore the particle changes direction only at
\[
\boxed{t=5}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for checking signs around the rest times.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for checking signs around the rest times.

rubric.description: 1 point for recognizing no sign change at \(t=2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for recognizing no sign change at \(t=2\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the direction change at \(t=5\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the direction change at \(t=5\).

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: What is the farthest to the left of the origin that the particle moves?
parts.answer:
The particle moves left until \(t=5\), then moves right. Thus its minimum position occurs at \(t=5\):
\[
x(5)=(5-2)^3(5-6)=27(-1)=-27.
\]
Therefore the farthest-left position is
\[
\boxed{x=-27},
\]
which is \(27\) units to the left of the origin.

rubric.description: 1 point for identifying the minimum at the direction-change time.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying the minimum at the direction-change time.

rubric.description: 1 point for evaluating \(x(5)\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for evaluating \(x(5)\).

rubric.description: 1 point for obtaining \(-27\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for obtaining \(-27\).

rubric.description: 1 point for interpreting this as 27 units left of the origin.

questions.prompt:
Let
\[
f(x)=k\sin(kx),
\]
where \(k\) is a positive constant. Consider one positive arch of the graph, from \(x=0\) to \(x=\frac{\pi}{k}\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the area of the region bounded by one arch of the graph of \(f\) and the \(x\)-axis.
parts.answer:
The area is
\[
\int_0^{\pi/k} k\sin(kx)\,dx.
\]
Let \(u=kx\), so \(du=k\,dx\). Then
\[
\int_0^{\pi}\sin u\,du=[-\cos u]_0^{\pi}=2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{A=2}.
\]

rubric.description: 1 point for correct bounds \(0\) and \(\pi/k\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for correct bounds \(0\) and \(\pi/k\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct definite integral.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct definite integral.

rubric.description: 1 point for a valid substitution or antiderivative.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a valid substitution or antiderivative.

rubric.description: 1 point for evaluating the endpoint values.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for evaluating the endpoint values.

rubric.description: 1 point for \(A=2\).
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for \(A=2\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the area of the triangle formed by the \(x\)-axis and the tangent lines to this arch at the two points where the graph crosses the \(x\)-axis.
parts.answer:
Since
\[
f'(x)=k^2\cos(kx),
\]
the tangent at \(x=0\) is \(y=k^2x\), and the tangent at \(x=\pi/k\) is
\[
y=-k^2\left(x-\frac{\pi}{k}\right).
\]
They intersect at
\[
x=\frac{\pi}{2k},\qquad y=\frac{\pi k}{2}.
\]
The triangle has base \(\pi/k\) and height \(\pi k/2\). Thus
\[
A_\triangle=\frac12\left(\frac{\pi}{k}\right)\left(\frac{\pi k}{2}\right)
=\boxed{\frac{\pi^2}{4}}.
\]

questions.prompt:
A man has \(340\) yards of fencing for two separate fields. One field is a rectangle twice as long as it is wide, and the other is a square. The square must contain at least \(100\) square yards, and the rectangle must contain at least \(800\) square yards.

Let \(x\) be the width of the rectangular field.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the maximum and minimum possible values of \(x\).
parts.answer:
The rectangle has dimensions \(x\) by \(2x\), so its perimeter is \(6x\). Let the square side be \(s\). Using all the fencing,
\[
6x+4s=340,
\qquad s=85-\frac32x.
\]
The rectangle-area constraint gives
\[
2x^2\ge800\quad\Rightarrow\quad x\ge20.
\]
The square-area constraint gives \(s^2\ge100\). Since \(s>0\), \(s\ge10\), so
\[
85-\frac32x\ge10\quad\Rightarrow\quad x\le50.
\]
Hence
\[
\boxed{20\le x\le50}.
\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: What is the greatest total number of square yards that can be enclosed in the two fields? Justify your answer.
parts.answer:
The total area is
\[
A(x)=2x^2+s^2=2x^2+\left(85-\frac32x\right)^2
=\frac{17}{4}x^2-255x+7225.
\]
Because \(A''(x)=\tfrac{17}{2}>0\), this quadratic is concave up, so its maximum on the closed interval \([20,50]\) occurs at an endpoint.
\[
A(20)=800+55^2=3825,
\]
\[
A(50)=5000+10^2=5100.
\]
Therefore the greatest possible total area is
\[
\boxed{5100\text{ square yards}},
\]
achieved with a \(50\times100\) rectangle and a \(10\times10\) square.
